short.headline: India is three countries: one that spends, one that earns, one that owns
short.dek: The same Indian top 1% takes 4.3% or 22.3% of income, depending on the lens. Consumption Gini is 25.5, income concentration is among the world’s highest, and wealth inequality is extreme.
short.body: Inequality in India is a paradox. The World Bank’s consumption Gini of 25.5 makes the country appear deeply equal; the World Inequality Lab’s estimates show the top 1% capturing 22.3% of income and 40.1% of wealth, levels starker than the colonial era. Both stories are true. They measure different things (spending, earning, owning) with different tools (doorstep surveys, tax records) that have opposite blind spots. This page walks the evidence, rung by rung: from the spending ladder and caste-wage gaps to the collapse of extreme poverty. The honest answer is that India is mildly unequal in consumption, highly unequal in income, and extremely unequal in wealth, all at once.

macha.heading: So is India equal or not, macha?
macha.body: If you only look at what people spend on groceries and rent—the consumption Gini—India looks like a flat society, and it’s been getting flatter. But spending is not earning. The moment you look at income, the picture flips: the top 1% has been pulling away since the 1980s, and by 2022 they were taking nearly a quarter of all pre-tax income. When you count wealth—land, gold, shares, minus loans—the concentration is even sharper. All three views are true. The trick is knowing which map you’re holding. Remember, surveys miss billionaires, and tax records don’t poll every household. The truth isn’t in one number; it’s in holding all three at once.
macha.soWhat: India is mildly unequal in what people spend, highly unequal in what they earn, and extremely unequal in what they own—and you need all three lenses to see it.

article.title: How Unequal Is India?
article.standfirst: India is mildly unequal in what people spend, highly unequal in what they earn, and extremely unequal in what they own, a seeming contradiction that comes from measuring inequality through different lenses with opposite blind spots.
article.bodyMarkdown:
## Why can't experts agree on whether India is equal?

Because they are not measuring the same thing, and both are right. Look at the two lines: they are both trying to capture the share of income going to India's richest 1%, and they are miles apart. The lower line comes from household surveys, knock on enough doors, ask what people earn and spend, add it up. The trouble is that a billionaire does not open the door to a survey enumerator, and if he does, he does not report his capital gains. So surveys see a flatter, more equal India almost by construction. The World Bank survey method estimates the top 1% income share at 4.3% in 2022, down from 5.9% in 1977.

The higher line starts from tax records and national accounts and works out where the rest of the income must have gone. It catches the top tail the survey misses. According to the World Inequality Database method, the top 1% took 22.3% of pre-tax national income in 2024, up from just 7.1% in 1980. Neither line is a lie. The distance between them — a top 1% share of 4% by one method, 22% by the other — is not a rounding error to average away; it is the measure of how much inequality the surveys cannot see.

## By what people spend, how unequal is India really?

A shopkeeper in Kanpur does not spend everything he earns. He saves for his children’s education, reinvests in the shop, maybe buys a small plot. His spending looks far more like his neighbour’s than his income does. This is why consumption inequality is always lower than income inequality, and in India it is strikingly low by global standards. The World Bank’s consumption Gini, a measure where 0 means perfect equality and 100 means one person spends everything, stood at 25.5 in 2022, down from 29.7 in 1977. That single number has anchored the story that India is an unusually equal society.

But the consumption lens is a mirror that only reflects the bottom and middle clearly. The very rich rarely show up in household surveys, and when they do, their spending tells you little about their true command over resources. So a low and falling consumption Gini is real, but it is a story about the floor and the middle, not the ceiling.

## What happens when we look at income instead of spending?

Imagine a salaried professional earning Rs 2 lakh a month who saves half, and a fund manager whose portfolio earns Rs 20 lakh in a bull run. The professional’s income is mostly salary; the fund manager’s is capital gains. Household surveys pick up the salary; they often miss the capital gain. Administrative methods, combining tax tabulations with national accounts, capture both, but at the cost of modelling who gets what. That is the World Inequality Lab’s approach.

By that measure, the top 10% of Indians captured 58.9% of pre-tax national income in 2022, up from 51.8% in 1923. The top 1% alone took 23.1% in 2022, up from a low of 7.1% in 1980. Both shares have risen steeply since liberalisation. These figures are hotly contested. Critics like Bhalla and Bhasin argue the method overstates top concentration by imputing income and wealth distributions that are not directly observed. The debate is genuine: the shape of the top tail depends heavily on what you assume about unreported income.

## And when we count what people own?

Consumption is a flow; wealth is the stock. A parched field and a penthouse in Worli are both visible, but their owners sit on vastly different accumulations of land, gold, shares, and property, minus whatever they owe. Wealth inequality is almost always the starkest dimension, because the rich can save, invest, and pass assets to the next generation.

In India, the top 1% held an estimated 40.1% of net personal wealth in 2024, up from 19.1% in 1820. The top 10% held 65%, up from 52.8%. These shares have not just risen; they have accelerated in the post-1991 period. Measuring wealth is even harder than measuring income: land values are opaque, gold is underreported, and billionaire fortunes swing with the stock market. The estimates carry uncertainty, but the direction and scale of concentration are hard to dispute.

## If consumption inequality is low, how does the spending actually split?

A Gini of 25.5 sounds abstract. The distributional data brings it to the ground. The poorest half of India’s population accounts for 32.8% of total consumption. The middle 40% accounts for 45%. The richest 10% accounts for 22.1%. In a perfectly equal world, the richest 10% would consume 10%. Here they consume more than twice that. But notice: the richest 10%’s consumption share has fallen from 25.2% in 1977, while the bottom 50%’s share has risen from 30.4%. The floor has nudged up, and the middle has widened.

These are consumption shares. If this were income or wealth, the gaps would be far larger. The chart shows that even the most equalising lens reveals a lopsided distribution.

## What does the gap between the richest and poorest fifths look like over time?

Focus on the richest 20% and the poorest 20%. In 1977, the top quintile consumed 39.1% of total spending, the bottom quintile just 9.1%. By 2022, those numbers had moved to 36.1% and 10.4%. The gap has narrowed slightly, but it remains enormous: the richest fifth still consumes 3.5 times as much as the poorest fifth.

The movement is glacial. Over 45 years, the bottom quintile gained just 1.3 percentage points of the consumption pie. This is not a story of convergence; it is a story of a stubbornly persistent spread.

## What does India’s own consumption survey say about inequality?

The Household Consumption Expenditure Survey (HCES), conducted by MoSPI, is India’s own yardstick. Its rural Gini fell from 28.3 in 2011-12 to 23.7 in 2023-24; the urban Gini fell more sharply, from 36.3 to 28.4. Both declines underpin the claim that inequality is falling.

There is a catch. The 2023-24 round introduced a methodological change: it imputed the value of free government rations and other welfare transfers into consumption. This raises reported spending, especially among the poor, mechanically reducing inequality. So part of the Gini drop is a genuine raising of the floor, and part is a change in what is being measured. Disentangling the two is a live puzzle, and the size of the drop likely overstates the improvement.

## What does the spending ladder look like, rung by rung?

A Gini compresses the whole distribution into one number; here are the actual rungs. In rural India, the poorest 0–5% of households spent Rs 1,677 per person per month, the next 5–10% Rs 2,126, and it rises step by step until the 80–90% group spends Rs 5,763. In urban India, the climb is steeper: the poorest 0–5% spend Rs 2,376, the 40–50% group Rs 5,622, and the 80–90% group Rs 10,139.

These rupee amounts put flesh on the numbers. The ratio between the top and bottom rungs is about 3.5 in rural and over 4 in urban, just from consumption differences. Before we count income or wealth.

## Is spending rising, and is the gap between town and country shrinking?

Both rural and urban average monthly per-capita consumption (MPCE) have risen sharply. Rural MPCE went from Rs 1,430 in 2011-12 to Rs 4,122 in 2023-24. Urban MPCE rose from Rs 2,630 to Rs 6,996. The urban-rural ratio narrowed from 1.84 (84% higher urban spend) to 1.70 (70% higher). The countryside caught up somewhat, partly because welfare transfers, now counted as consumption, disproportionately lifted the rural poor.

This rising floor is the engine that drove poverty down. But note: these are current rupees. After accounting for inflation, the real rise would be smaller. Still, the direction is clear.

## Does your state matter as much as your class?

A national Gini erases the map. Rural Kerala’s average MPCE in 2023-24 was Rs 6,611 per person per month; rural Gujarat’s was Rs 4,116. That 1.6-fold gap is comparable to differences between much poorer and much richer countries. Among urban areas, Telangana led at Rs 8,978, while Assam was at Rs 6,794.

This spatial inequality is invisible in any single national number. A person born in one state, even at the same income percentile, can have a vastly different spending level than a person born in another. The data also reminds us that averages within states mask internal divides by caste, class, and urban-rural location.

## How does caste shape what households spend?

Microdata from HCES 2023-24, which the official factsheet does not publish, reveals a clean gradient. A household headed by a person from the ‘Others’ (forward caste) category spends Rs 6,148 per person per month. An OBC household spends Rs 5,068 (18% less), a Scheduled Caste household Rs 4,277 (30% less), and a Scheduled Tribe household Rs 3,614 (41% less).

These are raw averages. Within each group, the Gini, ranging from 0.26 for Scheduled Castes to 0.31 for Others, shows that inequality exists inside as well as between. The caste gap persists even after accounting for education and occupation in deeper studies, but this chart simply lays out the factual spending ladder across social groups.

## Does religion matter in consumption?

The same microdata shows differences by religion of the household head. Hindu households spent Rs 5,089 per person per month on average. Muslim households spent Rs 4,583, about 10% below. Sikh and Christian households spent 28% and 20% above the Hindu average, at Rs 6,509 and Rs 6,111 respectively. Jain households had the highest average at Rs 6,509 (same as Sikh in the data).

These numbers reflect not just religion but the intersecting effects of region, caste composition, and historical occupational patterns. Sample sizes for smaller groups like Jains and Sikhs are small, so the estimates for them are noisier. But the broad pattern is consistent with other surveys.

## Does the same pattern show up in wages?

Consumption is the gap after the fact; wages are where it starts. Among regular-salaried workers, an ‘Others’ caste worker earned Rs 25,957 a month in 2023-24, while a Scheduled Caste worker earned Rs 16,516, a 36% pay gap. Scheduled Tribe workers earned Rs 18,343 (29% less), and OBC workers Rs 19,735 (24% less).

These figures come from the Periodic Labour Force Survey, not the HCES. They cover only regular-salaried employees, about a quarter of the workforce. The gap reflects differences in education, occupation, and sector, not direct discrimination per se. Still, the wage ladder mirrors the consumption ladder with remarkable fidelity.

## What happens when caste and gender stack together?

The two disadvantages compound. Among regular-salaried workers, a forward-caste woman earns 84% of what a forward-caste man earns. An OBC woman earns 73%. A Scheduled Caste or Scheduled Tribe woman earns just 63% of her male counterpart’s salary. The gender pay gap is widest exactly where the caste gap is widest.

The absolute amounts are even starker. A forward-caste man earns Rs 27,004; a forward-caste woman Rs 22,630. A Scheduled Caste man earns Rs 18,384; a Scheduled Caste woman Rs 11,665. The chart shows that inequality is not additive, it is multiplicative. The most disadvantaged group faces a double penalty.

## Do wages divide along religious lines too?

On the earnings side, the PLFS data shows Muslim regular-salaried workers at Rs 17,671 per month, compared to Rs 21,332 for Hindu workers. Christian workers earned Rs 25,094, Jain workers Rs 24,678, and Sikh workers Rs 17,055. The gaps are consistent with the consumption differences, though the Sikh figure here is lower than the consumption ranking, likely due to differences in sample and occupational mix.

As with consumption, religious wage gaps are influenced by regional concentration, urbanisation, and sectoral employment. These raw averages do not establish discrimination, but they show the structure of earnings across communities.

## What about extreme poverty? Has it fallen?

While the top has pulled away, the bottom has not stood still. The share of Indians living below the extreme poverty line of $2.15 a day (2017 PPP) has collapsed: from 59.7% in 1977 to 5.3% in 2022. The steepest falls occurred after 2004. In absolute numbers, hundreds of millions have moved above the most basic subsistence threshold.

This is the other half of the inequality story. The floor rose dramatically. But the ceiling rose faster. Falling extreme poverty and rising top-end inequality are both true at the same time. One does not cancel the other. The distance between the poorest and the richest has widened, even as acute destitution has shrunk.

editorialPlan.heroDescription: India is a single country with three starkly different inequality stories depending on whether you look at consumption, income, or wealth. The page walks through the data, from the consumption Gini of 25.5 to the top 1% income share of 22.3%, and down to the spending ladder by caste, religion, and gender, ending with poverty falling to 5.3%.

pullQuotes.quote: Tax records put India’s top 1% at 22% of national income; surveys say just 4%. That gap is the inequality the surveys cannot see.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Think of a pot of money shared among 100 people. If everyone gets exactly the same, the Gini is 0. If one person takes everything, the Gini is 100. It’s a single number that captures how evenly a resource is spread.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: The Gini is the most common measure of inequality, but here it changes completely depending on whether you use consumption, income, or wealth.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Consumption is what you actually spend on food, rent, and daily life, not what you earn. Rich households save a lot, so consumption inequality is naturally lower than income inequality. A Gini of 25.5 on consumption signals a relatively even distribution of what people report spending.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: India’s 'most equal' story is based on consumption data from surveys that miss the very rich.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Income includes salaries, business profits, and capital gains—the full flow of money before taxes. Surveys often miss the top tail because the rich either refuse to participate or under-report earnings from investments. Administrative methods like the World Inequality Lab try to fill that gap by using tax records and national accounts.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Income inequality in India, by the World Inequality Database, shows the top 1% taking over 22% of national income, a figure that has risen sharply since the 1980s.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Wealth is the stock of what you own minus debts—land, gold, shares, property. It accumulates over generations and grows through investment returns. Wealth inequality is almost always far higher than income inequality because the rich can save and compound their gains.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: India’s top 1% own an estimated 40% of net personal wealth, reflecting extreme concentration that dwarfs consumption inequality.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Take a household’s total spending in a month, divide by the number of members. That’s MPCE. It’s the backbone of India’s poverty and inequality measurement, because it directly reflects what people actually consume.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: HCES 2023-24 gives fresh MPCE numbers, and the distribution by fractiles, state, caste, and religion opens a window into inequality beyond the Gini.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: The World Bank’s international poverty line, set at $2.15 per day in 2017 purchasing-power-adjusted dollars. It represents a bare-minimum standard of living; anyone below it lacks basic subsistence.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: The dramatic fall from nearly 60% to just over 5% shows that the bottom has risen enormously, even as the top has pulled away.

chartExplainers.title: Same country, two verdicts on inequality
chartExplainers.takeaway: The World Inequality Database puts the top 1% income share at 22.3% in 2024; the World Bank’s survey method records just 4.3% in 2022.
chartExplainers.detail: Two lines attempt to measure the same thing: the share of pre-tax income going to the top 1% in India. The lower, flatter line comes from household surveys that miss the very rich, giving a 4.3% share in 2022. The higher, rapidly rising line draws on tax records and national accounts to capture the full top tail, reaching 22.3% in 2024. The survey line shows a slight decline since 1977, while the administrative line shows a quadrupling since 1980. The chasm between them is not an error; it is the measurement uncertainty at the heart of India’s inequality debate.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The whole debate fits in one frame: both lines try to measure the same concept, and they disagree sharply because they are built differently.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Two lines tracking top 1% income share over time. The lower line stays flat near 5%; the higher line climbs from 7% to 22%. The gap widens dramatically.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don’t assume one line is right and the other wrong. Each measures a different slice of the distribution with its own blind spots.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use large labels, sparse ticks, and a short caption.

chartExplainers.title: By what people spend, India looks equal — and more so
chartExplainers.takeaway: The World Bank’s consumption Gini fell from 29.7 in 1977 to 25.5 in 2022.
chartExplainers.detail: This single declining line is the source of India’s ‘most equal’ reputation. The Gini index runs from 0 (perfect equality) to 100 (one person spends everything). A value of 25.5 is low by global standards, and the direction has been consistently downward since the 1970s. Because rich households save and invest most of their extra income, their spending looks much closer to the average, compressing the distribution. The chart uses consumption — what households actually report spending — not income or wealth, so it naturally understates the full extent of inequality, especially at the very top.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The consumption Gini anchors the narrative that India is an unusually equal country.
chartExplainers.howToRead: A single line falling from 1977 to 2022. Lower is less unequal. The decline has been gentle but persistent.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: A low consumption Gini does not mean India is an equal society — only that spending is relatively even, and the very rich are largely absent from this measure.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a clean line chart with prominent labels.

chartExplainers.title: By what people earn, the top is pulling away
chartExplainers.takeaway: The top 10% captured 58.9% of pre-tax national income in 2022, the top 1% 23.1%.
chartExplainers.detail: Two lines surge upward from the 1980s. The top 10% share, which was 51.8% in 1923, climbs to 58.9% in 2022. The top 1% share goes from 7.1% in 1980 to 23.1% in 2022 — a more than threefold increase. This data, built from tax records and national accounts by the World Inequality Lab, includes income sources like capital gains that surveys miss. The result is that India’s income concentration today rivals or exceeds the levels estimated for the colonial era. The method is contested; critics argue that imputing the full distribution can overstate the top tail. Still, the trend is unmistakable.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Income tells the opposite story to consumption: the top has pulled away sharply since liberalisation.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Two lines: the top 10% (upper) and top 1% (lower). Both rise steeply from the early 1980s.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: These are pre-tax shares and modelled estimates, not direct observations. They show a direction, not an exact number.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a multi-line chart with clear colour coding.

chartExplainers.title: By what people own, concentration is extreme
chartExplainers.takeaway: The top 1% held 40.1% of net personal wealth in 2024, the top 10% 65%.
chartExplainers.detail: Wealth is the most concentrated of the three dimensions. The top 10%’s share has risen from 52.8% in 1820 to 65% in 2024; the top 1%’s share from 19.1% to 40.1% over the same period. The post-1991 acceleration is dramatic. Wealth includes land, gold, financial assets, and property, minus debts. Because wealth earns returns and is bequeathed across generations, convergence is extremely slow. The estimates here are based on a mix of sources, and uncertainty around the extreme tail is high. But even allowing for measurement error, the degree of concentration is striking.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Wealth is where inequality is starkest, and it matters because wealth determines long-term economic power.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Two lines: the top 10% (upper) and top 1% (lower). Both rise, with the post-1991 upward bend visible.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Wealth data is harder to capture than income; the very top may be under- or over-estimated, but the general pattern of high concentration is robust.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a multi-line chart; annotate the latest values.

chartExplainers.title: How consumption splits, bottom half to top tenth
chartExplainers.takeaway: The poorest 50% account for 32.8% of total consumption, the richest 10% for 22.1%.
chartExplainers.detail: Three bars bring the Gini to life. The bottom half of the population consumes just under one-third of all spending, the middle 40% gets 45%, and the top 10% gets 22.1%. In a perfectly equal world, each group’s share would match its population share — the poorest 50% would get 50%, the richest 10% 10%. The actual distribution is skewed, but far less so than income or wealth. The richest 10%’s share here has fallen from 25.2% in 1977, while the bottom 50%’s has risen from 30.4%. The floor has gained, but the top still far outstrips its proportional share.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Even under the most equalising lens, the distribution is lopsided, and this chart shows how the pie is actually divided.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Three bars showing the percentage of total consumption going to each group. Compare the richest 10% bar to the poorest 50%.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: This is consumption only; the gaps would be far larger for income and wealth.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a simple bar chart with large labels.

chartExplainers.title: The richest fifth vs the poorest fifth
chartExplainers.takeaway: The top 20% consumed 36.1% of total spending in 2022, the bottom 20% 10.4%, a gap that has narrowed only marginally since 1977.
chartExplainers.detail: Two lines track the consumption shares of the richest and poorest quintiles over 45 years. The top 20% started at 39.1% in 1977 and fell to 36.1%. The bottom 20% moved from 9.1% to 10.4%. The gap remains wide: the richest fifth spends 3.5 times as much as the poorest fifth. The movement is slow — a 1.3 percentage point gain for the poorest over nearly half a century. This visual tempers the optimistic read of the Gini decline: the bottom has barely inched up its share of the pie.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: A simple, durable gap that shows how little the relative position of the poorest has changed.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Two lines over time. The top line is the richest 20%; the bottom line, the poorest 20%. The vertical distance is the gap.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: A narrowing gap does not mean absolute equality; the richest still outspend the poorest massively.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a dual-line chart with annotations for latest values.

chartExplainers.title: Town and country, and the survey behind the 'falling' claim
chartExplainers.takeaway: Rural Gini fell from 28.3 to 23.7, urban from 36.3 to 28.4 between 2011–12 and 2023–24, but the latest round includes imputed welfare transfers.
chartExplainers.detail: India’s own HCES shows consumption inequality declining in both rural and urban areas. The rural Gini, already lower, fell 4.6 points; the urban Gini dropped 7.9 points. However, the 2023-24 survey added the imputed value of free rations and other welfare items to consumption, which raises reported spending among the poor and mechanically reduces measured inequality. So the decline is partly a real improvement in living standards and partly a statistical artefact. The true fall is smaller than these numbers suggest, but the direction is likely real.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: This is the core of the 'inequality is declining' story from official Indian data, but it comes with an important methodology caveat.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Two lines, rural and urban Gini indices. Both fall between the 2011–12 and 2023–24 survey rounds.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don’t treat the full decline as purely real. The introduction of imputation means some of the drop is a change in measurement.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a multi-line chart with a note about the imputation change.

chartExplainers.title: The spending ladder, rung by rung
chartExplainers.takeaway: In rural India, the poorest 0–5% spend Rs 1,677 per person per month, while the 80–90% fractile spends Rs 5,763; urban gaps are wider.
chartExplainers.detail: Instead of a single Gini, this chart shows the actual MPCE at each step of the distribution. In rural areas, spending rises gradually from Rs 1,677 for the poorest 5% to Rs 5,763 for the 80–90% group. Urban spending starts higher and climbs steeper: from Rs 2,376 for the bottom 5% to Rs 10,139 for the 80–90% group. The step pattern makes the gap tangible. Even within consumption, the richest decile spends 3–4 times more than the poorest. These differences, before we factor in income and wealth, are visible in daily life — from the quality of food to the schooling of children.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The Gini compresses the distribution; this ladder shows the actual rupee rungs that households stand on.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Horizontal bars with rupee values for each fractile class. Compare the bottom bars to the top.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: This is one year only, and each bar is an average; individuals within a fractile vary.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use directional horizontal bars, sparing and large labels.

chartExplainers.title: Spending is rising, and the town-country gap is narrowing
chartExplainers.takeaway: Rural MPCE rose from Rs 1,430 to Rs 4,122, urban from Rs 2,630 to Rs 6,996 between 2011–12 and 2023–24; the urban-rural ratio fell from 1.84 to 1.70.
chartExplainers.detail: Average monthly per-capita consumption has nearly tripled in both rural and urban India over 12 years in current rupees. More importantly, the gap between the two shrunk. In 2011–12, the average urban person spent 84% more than the average rural person; by 2023–24, that excess had fallen to 70%. The narrowing partly reflects the imputation of welfare transfers, which disproportionately raised rural spending, but it also signals that rural incomes and welfare have genuinely risen. This convergence is one of the engines behind the sharp decline in extreme poverty.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The reason poverty fell is that average spending rose everywhere, and rural areas caught up somewhat.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Two upward-sloping lines. The urban line is higher but the gap narrows over time.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Current rupees do not adjust for inflation; real growth is smaller. Also, the imputation in 2023–24 inflates the apparent rise.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Two lines with a caption explaining the gap.

chartExplainers.title: Which India you live in matters as much as which class
chartExplainers.takeaway: Rural Kerala’s MPCE of Rs 6,611 is more than 1.6 times that of rural Gujarat’s Rs 4,116.
chartExplainers.detail: A national Gini hides enormous state-level variation. In rural areas, the top states — Kerala, Punjab, Tamil Nadu — spend about Rs 5,500–6,600 per person per month. At the lower end, among the states shown, Gujarat’s rural MPCE is Rs 4,116. Urban areas show a similar spread: Telangana leads at Rs 8,978, while Assam is at Rs 6,794. The gap between the richest and poorest state is comparable to differences between much poorer and much richer countries. This spatial inequality means that where you are born in India shapes your living standards as deeply as your class or caste.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: A national average erases the map; this chart puts the vast regional disparities in focus.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Horizontal bars showing average rural MPCE by state, sorted highest to lowest. Compare the extreme ends.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: State averages hide within-state inequality. Also, the urban ranking is different from rural.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use horizontal bars with large state labels.

chartExplainers.title: The consumption gap that runs along caste
chartExplainers.takeaway: Scheduled Tribe households spend 41% less per person than 'Others' households (Rs 3,614 vs Rs 6,148).
chartExplainers.detail: Microdata from HCES 2023-24 reveals a clean caste gradient in average MPCE. At the top, ‘Others’ (forward caste) households spend Rs 6,148 per person per month. OBC households spend Rs 5,068 (18% less), Scheduled Caste households Rs 4,277 (30% less), and Scheduled Tribe households Rs 3,614 (41% less). Within each group, the Gini ranges from 0.26 for SCs to 0.31 for Others, showing that inequality exists inside categories too. The raw averages do not adjust for education or occupation, but they capture the cumulative effect of historical disadvantage.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The official factsheet doesn’t publish this, but the microdata shows a clear caste-based consumption hierarchy.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Horizontal bars for each caste group. The length shows the average MPCE; the gap from the top bar is the absolute inequality.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Population shares differ; OBCs are the largest group. Internal inequality (Gini) is also high.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Horizontal bars with percentage gap annotations.

chartExplainers.title: And along religion
chartExplainers.takeaway: Muslim households spend Rs 4,583 per person per month, 10% below the Hindu average; Sikh and Christian households spend 20–28% above.
chartExplainers.detail: Consumption differs by religion of the household head. The Hindu average is Rs 5,089; Muslim households spend Rs 4,583 (10% less). Sikh (Rs 6,509) and Christian (Rs 6,111) households spend substantially more, while Buddhist households (Rs 4,912) are slightly below. These gaps reflect a mix of regional concentration, occupational patterns, and historical factors. Sample sizes for smaller groups like Sikhs and Buddhists are small, so their estimates are less precise. But the ranking is consistent with other surveys.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The same microdata shows religion-based variation in consumption, an often-overlooked dimension of inequality.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Horizontal bars for each religious group, with the Hindu average marked as reference.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Small sample sizes make the estimates for minority groups noisy. These are raw averages, not evidence of discrimination.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Horizontal bars with clear group labels.

chartExplainers.title: The same gap in what a job pays
chartExplainers.takeaway: Among regular-salaried workers, a Scheduled Caste worker earns Rs 16,516 per month, 36% less than an 'Others' caste worker at Rs 25,957.
chartExplainers.detail: Wages from the Periodic Labour Force Survey show a caste hierarchy that mirrors the consumption ladder. ‘Others’ caste workers earn the most, followed by OBCs (Rs 19,735, 24% gap), Scheduled Tribes (Rs 18,343, 29% gap), and Scheduled Castes (Rs 16,516, 36% gap). These are regular-salaried workers only, about a quarter of the workforce. The gap reflects differences in education, occupation, and sector — it is not a pure measure of discrimination. But it is a starting point: before consumption patterns or wealth, the pay packet already differs sharply by caste.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Consumption is the gap after the fact; wages are where it begins, and the caste pay gap is large and systematic.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Horizontal bars for each caste group, with percentage difference from 'Others' noted.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: This is limited to regular-salaried workers; the self-employed and casual labourers are excluded.

chartExplainers.title: Caste and gender stack on top of each other
chartExplainers.takeaway: Scheduled Caste and Scheduled Tribe women earn just 63% of their male counterparts’ salary; forward-caste women earn 84%.
chartExplainers.detail: The gender pay gap among regular-salaried workers varies sharply by caste. For ‘Others’, women earn 84% of what men do — a gap, but relatively narrow. For OBCs, the ratio drops to 73%. For Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes, it falls to 63%. The absolute figures are even more telling: a forward-caste man earns Rs 27,004, a forward-caste woman Rs 22,630; a Scheduled Caste man earns Rs 18,384, a Scheduled Caste woman Rs 11,665. The two disadvantages compound: the gender gap is widest exactly where the caste gap is widest.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Inequality is not additive; it is multiplicative. Caste and gender together create the deepest pay divide.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Horizontal bars showing women’s wage as a percentage of men’s, within each caste group. Lower bar = wider gap.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: The percentage hides absolute levels. Both men and women from disadvantaged groups earn less, so the 63% is a fraction of a smaller base.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use horizontal bars with percentage labels and male/female wage annotations.

chartExplainers.title: And wages split by religion too
chartExplainers.takeaway: Muslim regular-salaried workers earn Rs 17,671 per month, compared to Rs 21,332 for Hindu workers.
chartExplainers.detail: The PLFS data shows average monthly wages for regular-salaried workers by religion. Christian workers earn the most (Rs 25,094), followed by Jain (Rs 24,678) and Buddhist (Rs 23,940). Hindu workers earn Rs 21,332. Muslim workers earn Rs 17,671, and Sikh workers Rs 17,055. These raw averages do not control for occupation, education, or region. The lower average for Sikhs, for example, may partly reflect the high proportion of Sikh workers in manufacturing or blue-collar roles. The Muslim wage gap, consistently about 17% below the Hindu average, aligns with other surveys.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Wage inequalities run along religious lines too, reflecting complex intersections of region, caste, and occupation.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Horizontal bars by religion, with the Hindu bar as reference.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Small sample sizes for some groups; these are raw averages, not causal evidence of discrimination.

chartExplainers.title: The other half: extreme poverty fell sharply
chartExplainers.takeaway: Extreme poverty ($2.15/day) fell from 59.7% in 1977 to 5.3% in 2022.
chartExplainers.detail: One single line tells the story of the bottom rising. In 1977, nearly six in ten Indians lived below the absolute poverty line. By 2022, that share had collapsed to one in twenty. The steepest decline occurred after 2004, corresponding to a period of high growth and expanded welfare. This does not mean inequality fell; it means the floor rose dramatically. The top continues to pull away, but the condition of the poorest has improved vastly. Both facts can be true, and both are present in the data.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: The final chart shows that the other half of the story — the rising floor — is real and dramatic.
chartExplainers.howToRead: A single line dropping from 1977 to 2022. The y-axis is percentage of population below $2.15/day.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: This is an absolute poverty line, not a measure of inequality. Falling poverty and rising top-end inequality can and do coexist.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A simple line chart with a steep downward trend; annotate the latest value.

editorialPlanDraft.answerThesis: India is mildly unequal in what people spend, highly unequal in what they earn, and extremely unequal in what they own—a seeming contradiction that comes from measuring inequality through different lenses with opposite blind spots.
requiredConcepts: Inequality is not one number: a Gini (0 = everyone equal, 100 = one person has everything) or a top share can be computed on consumption, on income, or on wealth, and the three give very different answers for India because they measure different things

Consumption inequality is lowest and is what the World Bank's '25.5, fourth-most-equal' headline measures; it is based on what households spend, and spending is naturally more equal than income because the rich save and invest most of their extra income rather than consuming it

Income inequality is much higher than consumption inequality and has been rising; the World Inequality Lab estimates the top 1% take about 22.6% of national income (2022-23), among the highest in the world
Wealth inequality is the most extreme of the three; the top 1% are estimated to own about 40.1% of net personal wealth and the bottom 50% only about 6.4%

Household surveys (the basis of the World Bank consumption Gini and India's HCES) systematically miss the very rich — they refuse to participate or under-report — which mechanically lowers measured inequality; this is a known limitation the World Bank itself flags, not a conspiracy

The World Inequality Lab method combines tax tabulations, national accounts and rich lists to estimate the full distribution including the top tail; this captures capital income surveys miss, but it is a MODELLED estimate, not a direct observation, and has been contested

The Bhalla–Bhasin critique (2024) argues the World Inequality Lab does not observe the same individuals' income and wealth and instead imputes the full distribution, which they say overstates top concentration; present this as a genuine expert disagreement, not a settled question

India's HCES (Household Consumption Expenditure Survey) 2022-23 and 2023-24 show the consumption Gini falling in both rural and urban areas; but part of the 2023-24 fall reflects a methodology change — newly imputing the value of free government rations and welfare into consumption — so it partly reflects a real rise in the floor and partly a change in what is being measured

Falling extreme poverty and rising top-end inequality are both true at the same time: the floor rose (fewer people below $2.15/day) even as the ceiling rose faster, so 'poverty fell' is not evidence that 'inequality fell'

These are national figures and hide large gaps by state, caste, religion and gender that this page's data does not fully capture; wealth-survey gaps (AIDIS) and unit-level microdata are follow-ups, so be honest about what is and is not shown

sectionPlan.heading: Why can't experts agree on whether India is equal?
sectionPlan.job: Explain that the paradox arises because two different methods (household survey vs tax-administrative) produce two incompatible answers for the top 1% income share, and both are technically correct within their own assumptions.
lockedNumbersToUse: Top 1% income share — World Inequality Database method, latest
Top 1% income share — World Inequality Database method, earliest
Top 1% income share — World Bank (survey) method, latest
Top 1% income share — World Bank (survey) method, earliest
conceptsToExplain: Top 1% income share can be measured by knocking on doors (survey) or by using tax records and national accounts (administrative).
Surveys miss the very rich, who are hard to reach and often under-report income.
Administrative methods capture capital income but rely on modelled assumptions to allocate unreported income.
sectionPlan.caveat: Neither line is a lie; the distance between them is the most honest thing on the page.

sectionPlan.heading: By what people spend, how unequal is India really?
sectionPlan.job: Show that consumption-based inequality, measured by the World Bank's Gini, has declined over time and is now very low by global standards, giving the 'most equal' narrative. Explain why consumption is naturally less unequal than income.
lockedNumbersToUse: Gini index — consumption (World Bank), latest
Gini index — consumption (World Bank), earliest
conceptsToExplain: The Gini index ranges from 0 (perfect equality) to 100 (perfect inequality).
Consumption is what households actually spend; rich households save and invest a larger share of their income, so spending inequality is lower than income inequality.
sectionPlan.caveat: The consumption Gini misses the very top because surveys undercount the rich; it captures the floor better than the ceiling.

sectionPlan.heading: What happens when we look at income instead of spending?
sectionPlan.job: Introduce the World Inequality Lab's income share estimates, showing that the top 1% and top 10% have captured a rising share of pre-tax national income, reaching levels higher than the colonial era.
lockedNumbersToUse: Income share of the top 1%, before tax (WID), latest
Income share of the top 1%, before tax (WID), earliest
Income share of the top 10%, before tax (WID), latest
Income share of the top 10%, before tax (WID), earliest
conceptsToExplain: Pre-tax national income includes all income before taxes and transfers.
Administrative methods include capital gains and dividends that surveys miss, but require modelling the full distribution.
sectionPlan.caveat: The WID estimates are modelled and have been contested by scholars like Bhalla and Bhasin, who argue the method overstates top concentration.

sectionPlan.heading: And when we count what people own?
sectionPlan.job: Present wealth inequality as the most extreme dimension, with the top 1% holding roughly 40% of net personal wealth. Explain that wealth builds on past income and investment returns, magnifying the gap.
lockedNumbersToUse: Wealth share of the richest 1% (WID), latest
Wealth share of the richest 1% (WID), earliest
Wealth share of the richest 10% (WID), latest
Wealth share of the richest 10% (WID), earliest
conceptsToExplain: Net personal wealth includes financial assets, real estate, and other valuables minus debts.
Wealth inequality is almost always higher than income inequality because the rich can save and compound returns over generations.
sectionPlan.caveat: Wealth estimates are even harder to measure than income; data on the very richest is especially uncertain.

sectionPlan.heading: If consumption inequality is low, how does the spending actually split?
sectionPlan.job: Translate the single Gini number into the actual shares of national consumption going to the bottom 50%, middle 40%, and top 10%, showing that even on spending the gap is large.
lockedNumbersToUse: Consumption share of the poorest 50% (World Bank distribution), latest
Consumption share of the middle 40% (World Bank distribution), latest
Consumption share of the richest 10% (World Bank distribution), latest
conceptsToExplain: A low Gini does not mean everyone spends the same; the richest 10% still consume about twice their 'fair share'.
sectionPlan.caveat: These are consumption shares, not income or wealth; the gaps would be larger for those measures.

sectionPlan.heading: What does the gap between the richest and poorest fifths look like over time?
sectionPlan.job: Show the long-run trend of the consumption share of the top 20% versus the bottom 20%, revealing a narrow but persistent gap that has shrunk slightly since the 1970s.
lockedNumbersToUse: Consumption share of the richest 20% (World Bank), latest
Consumption share of the richest 20% (World Bank), earliest
Consumption share of the poorest 20% (World Bank), latest
Consumption share of the poorest 20% (World Bank), earliest
conceptsToExplain: The richest quintile (top 20%) consumes about 3.5 times as much as the poorest quintile (bottom 20%).
sectionPlan.caveat: This is a consumption-based comparison only, and the share of the bottom 20% has increased only marginally in five decades.

sectionPlan.heading: What does India's own consumption survey say about inequality?
sectionPlan.job: Introduce the HCES rural and urban Gini series, showing a decline in both areas in the 2011-12 to 2023-24 period, which underpins the 'inequality is falling' claim. Flag the methodology change with imputation.
lockedNumbersToUse: Consumption Gini — rural (HCES/NSS), latest
Consumption Gini — rural (HCES/NSS), earliest
Consumption Gini — urban (HCES/NSS), latest
Consumption Gini — urban (HCES/NSS), earliest
conceptsToExplain: The HCES is India's own large-scale household consumption survey, used to measure poverty and inequality.
Imputation of free welfare items (like ration) into consumption increases reported spending, especially for the poor, reducing measured inequality.
sectionPlan.caveat: Part of the Gini decline in 2023-24 is real (floor rose) and part is a measurement change (counting items that weren't counted before). The exact split is debated.

sectionPlan.heading: What does the spending ladder look like, rung by rung?
sectionPlan.job: Display the HCES fractile MPCE data, showing actual rupees per person per month for each 5% or 10% group, making the inequality tangible.
lockedNumbersToUse: Rural average MPCE by fractile class, 2023-24: value (lowest and highest fractiles)
Urban average MPCE by fractile class, 2023-24: value (lowest and highest fractiles)
conceptsToExplain: Fractile classes split the population into groups ranked by spending; the poorest 5% spend X, the richest 5% spend Y.
sectionPlan.caveat: These are before imputation; imputed values would narrow the gap slightly.

sectionPlan.heading: Is spending rising, and is the gap between town and country shrinking?
sectionPlan.job: Show the growth in average MPCE for rural and urban areas, and note the narrowing ratio (urban/rural) as rural spending catches up. Connect this to the fall in poverty.
lockedNumbersToUse: Rural average MPCE (HCES, without imputation), latest
Rural average MPCE (HCES, without imputation), earliest
Urban average MPCE (HCES, without imputation), latest
Urban average MPCE (HCES, without imputation), earliest
conceptsToExplain: MPCE is Monthly Per Capita Consumption Expenditure, a proxy for material living standards.
A narrowing urban-rural ratio means the countryside is becoming less poor relative to cities.
sectionPlan.caveat: This is in current prices; real growth after inflation would be lower.

sectionPlan.heading: Does your state matter as much as your class?
sectionPlan.job: Show state-wise MPCE for rural and urban areas, highlighting large regional disparities (e.g., Kerala vs Chhattisgarh). Argue that location is a major dimension of inequality.
lockedNumbersToUse: Rural average MPCE by major state, 2023-24: value (top and bottom states)
Urban average MPCE by major state, 2023-24: value (top and bottom states)
conceptsToExplain: Average consumption in the richest state can be more than double that of the poorest state, a gap comparable to differences between countries.
sectionPlan.caveat: This is state average; within-state inequality also matters but is not shown here.

sectionPlan.heading: How does caste shape what households spend?
sectionPlan.job: Present MPCE by caste group from HCES microdata, showing a clear gradient: Others > OBC > SC > ST. Highlight the 41% gap between Others and ST.
lockedNumbersToUse: Consumption (MPCE) by caste group, 2023-24: value (all groups)
Consumption (MPCE) by caste group, 2023-24: gapVsOthersPct (SC, ST)
conceptsToExplain: Caste categories (Others, OBC, SC, ST) are self-reported in the survey; 'Others' is effectively the forward-caste group.
The gap persists even after adjusting for education and occupation, but this chart only shows raw averages.
sectionPlan.caveat: This is national average; within each group the Gini shows significant internal inequality as well.

sectionPlan.heading: Does religion matter in consumption?
sectionPlan.job: Show MPCE by religion, noting Jain and Sikh households above average, Muslim and Buddhist below. Keep the discussion measured and rooted in data.
lockedNumbersToUse: Consumption (MPCE) by religion, 2023-24: value (all groups)
Consumption (MPCE) by religion, 2023-24: gapVsHinduPct (Muslim, Sikh, Christian)
conceptsToExplain: Religious group is self-reported; consumption gaps may reflect a mix of historical, regional, and educational factors.
GapVsHinduPct is the percentage difference from Hindu average MPCE.
sectionPlan.caveat: Smaller religious groups (Jain, Buddhist, Sikh) have small sample sizes; their Ginis are based on fewer observations.

sectionPlan.heading: Does the same pattern show up in wages?
sectionPlan.job: Present regular-salaried wages by caste from PLFS, showing that the pay gap mirrors the consumption gap but is wider: Others earn 57% more than SCs.
lockedNumbersToUse: Regular-salaried monthly wage by caste, 2023-24: value (all groups)
Regular-salaried monthly wage by caste, 2023-24: gapVsOthersPct (SC, ST, OBC)
conceptsToExplain: Regular-salaried workers are a subset of the workforce; casual and self-employed workers are not included.
The wage gap is not a measure of discrimination per se; it reflects differences in education, occupation, and sector.
sectionPlan.caveat: PLFS captures only reported earnings; actual earnings may be higher or lower.

sectionPlan.heading: What happens when caste and gender stack together?
sectionPlan.job: Show the gender pay gap within each caste group, revealing that disadvantages compound: SC/ST women earn only 63% of what their male counterparts earn, while Others women earn 84%.
lockedNumbersToUse: Women's pay as a share of men's, by caste, 2023-24: value (all groups)
conceptsToExplain: The gender pay ratio is female average wage divided by male average wage within the same caste category for regular-salaried workers.
A lower ratio means a wider gender gap.
sectionPlan.caveat: This is limited to regular-salaried workers; the ratio might differ for casual or self-employed.

sectionPlan.heading: Do wages divide along religious lines too?
sectionPlan.job: Show average wages by religion for regular-salaried workers, noting that Muslim workers earn less than Hindu workers, while Christian and Jain earn more. Keep factual.
lockedNumbersToUse: Regular-salaried monthly wage by religion, 2023-24: value (all groups)
conceptsToExplain: Earnings gaps by religion can reflect differences in education, region, and sectoral employment.
sectionPlan.caveat: Causality cannot be inferred; these are raw averages.

sectionPlan.heading: What about extreme poverty? Has it fallen?
sectionPlan.job: Close the page by showing the extreme poverty rate ($2.15/day) falling from 59.7% in 1977 to 5.3% in 2022, and argue that both rising top inequality and falling poverty are true simultaneously.
lockedNumbersToUse: Extreme poverty headcount ($2.15/day, 2017 PPP), latest
Extreme poverty headcount ($2.15/day, 2017 PPP), earliest
conceptsToExplain: Extreme poverty line ($2.15/day) is an internationally comparable absolute threshold.
Falling poverty lifts the floor, but if the top grows faster, inequality can still rise.
sectionPlan.caveat: The survey data under the 2017 PPP revision may not capture some informal transfers, and the latest estimate is from 2022.

chartPlan.title: Same country, two verdicts on inequality
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The whole debate fits in one frame: both lines try to measure the share of income going to the richest 1%, and they disagree sharply because they are built differently.
chartPlan.howToRead: The two lines track the same concept (top 1% income share) over time, but one stays low and flat (survey method) while the other rises steeply (tax method). The gap between them is the measurement chasm.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't assume one line is 'correct' and the other 'wrong'. They both have strengths and weaknesses; the truth likely lies somewhere in between, but the gap itself reveals the depth of the uncertainty.

chartPlan.title: By what people spend, India looks equal — and more so
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The 'most equal' headline comes from here: a consumption Gini that fell to 25.5 in 2022-23, among the lowest in the world.
chartPlan.howToRead: The Gini index is a single number between 0 and 100. 0 means perfect equality (everyone spends the same); 100 means one person spends everything. The line falls, so inequality by this measure has been declining.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: A low consumption Gini does not mean India is an equal society—only that what households report spending is not very spread out. The very rich don't spend much of their income, so their consumption doesn't show in this measure.

chartPlan.title: By what people earn, the top is pulling away
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Income tells the opposite story to consumption: the share captured by the top 10% and top 1% has climbed back to, and past, colonial-era highs.
chartPlan.howToRead: Both lines (top 10% and top 1%) surge from the early 1980s onward. The top 1% share more than triples from 7.1% to 22.3%. The top 10% now takes nearly 59% of all pre-tax income.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: These are pre-tax shares; they don't reflect redistribution. Also, the series is modelled, not a direct observation, so exact values are debated.

chartPlan.title: By what people own, concentration is extreme
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Wealth is where inequality is starkest: a small group owns a very large share of everything India has accumulated.
chartPlan.howToRead: The top 1% line rises from around 20% in the 1980s to over 40% recently. The top 10% holds about 65% of net personal wealth.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Wealth data is even more uncertain than income, especially for the very rich. The series is based on estimates that combine many sources, and critics point to potential overestimation.

chartPlan.title: How consumption splits, bottom half to top tenth
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The flip side of a low Gini: even on consumption, the poorest half and the richest tenth spend strikingly different amounts.
chartPlan.howToRead: The bars show the share of total consumption going to each group in the latest survey. The bottom 50% of the population accounts for only about 33% of total spending.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: These shares don't translate directly to welfare—the same share can mean very different absolute amounts for different groups.

chartPlan.title: The richest fifth vs the poorest fifth
chartPlan.whyShowThis: A simple, durable gap: how much of national consumption the top fifth takes versus the bottom fifth, and how little it has moved.
chartPlan.howToRead: The top line (richest 20%) has drifted down from 39% to 36% over 45 years; the bottom line (poorest 20%) has inched up from 9% to 10.4%. The gap remains wide.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: This is a ratio; small absolute improvements for the poorest can still mean a large share gap.

chartPlan.title: Town and country, and the survey behind the 'falling' claim
chartPlan.whyShowThis: India's own consumption survey shows the Gini falling in both rural and urban areas—the basis for the 'inequality is declining' story.
chartPlan.howToRead: Both lines drop from 2011-12 to 2023-24. Urban inequality remains higher than rural (28.4 vs 23.7), but the urban drop was sharper.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: The 2023-24 figure includes imputed value of free government rations, which artificially lowers measured inequality. Compare with earlier rounds cautiously.

chartPlan.title: The spending ladder, rung by rung
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The Gini compresses the whole distribution into one number; this shows the actual rungs. The poorest 5% of rural India spends about ₹1,677 per person a month while the richest 5% spends ₹10,137—on consumption alone, before income or wealth widen the gap.
chartPlan.howToRead: Each bar represents the average MPCE of a 5% or 10% slice of the population, ranked from poorest to richest. The step pattern reveals how quickly spending rises across the distribution.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: This is one year only (2023-24) and average; individuals within each group vary widely.

chartPlan.title: Spending is rising, and the town-country gap is narrowing
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The reason poverty fell: average consumption rose for both rural and urban India, and the urban-rural gap shrank from 84% in 2011-12 to about 70% in 2023-24.
chartPlan.howToRead: Both lines slope upward. The urban line stays above the rural line but the gap between them narrows, meaning rural spending grew faster.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: This is in current rupees; real growth after inflation would be lower. Also, the narrowing gap is partly due to the imputation of welfare items in the latest round.

chartPlan.title: Which India you live in matters as much as which class
chartPlan.whyShowThis: A national Gini erases the map. Rural Kerala spends ₹6,611 per person a month—more than twice rural Chhattisgarh's ₹2,739. The gap between Indian states rivals the gap between countries, and it is invisible in any single national number.
chartPlan.howToRead: Horizontal bars show state averages; the length shows relative spending. The distance between the top and bottom state is the regional inequality gap.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: State averages hide within-state disparities. Also, urban state rankings differ from rural.

chartPlan.title: The consumption gap that runs along caste
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The factsheet never publishes this, but the microdata does: average consumption falls in a clean caste gradient. A Scheduled Tribe household spends about ₹3,614 per person a month against ₹6,148 for 'Others'—a 41% gap that no single national Gini reveals.
chartPlan.howToRead: Bars represent group averages. The length shows the rupee amount; the gap between bars is the absolute inequality.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Population shares differ; OBCs are the largest group. Also, within-group inequality is high (see Git data).

chartPlan.title: And along religion
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The same microdata splits consumption by religion: Jain and Sikh households spend well above the Hindu average, while Muslim households spend about 10% below it.
chartPlan.howToRead: Bars show group average MPCE. Comparing against the Hindu average (₹5,089) shows which groups are above or below.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Small sample sizes for Jain, Sikh, Buddhist make group averages noisy. Also, religion overlaps with region and caste.

chartPlan.title: The same gap in what a job pays
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Consumption is the gap after the fact; wages are where it starts. Among regular salaried workers, a forward-caste ('Others') worker earns about ₹25,957 a month against ₹16,516 for a Scheduled Caste worker—a 36% pay gap inside the same kind of job.
chartPlan.howToRead: Bars represent average monthly wage for regular salaried workers by caste group. The gapVsOthersPct shows the percentage difference from 'Others'.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: This only includes regular-salaried workers (about 25% of the workforce). Self-employed and casual workers are excluded.

chartPlan.title: Caste and gender stack on top of each other
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The two disadvantages compound. A forward-caste woman earns 84% of what a forward-caste man does; a Scheduled Caste or Scheduled Tribe woman earns just 63% of her male counterpart. The gender pay gap is widest exactly where the caste gap is widest.
chartPlan.howToRead: Each bar shows women's average wage as a percentage of men's within the same caste group. A lower bar means a bigger gender gap.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: The ratio hides absolute levels: both men and women from SC/ST earn less than Others, so the 63% is a fraction of a smaller base.

chartPlan.title: And wages split by religion too
chartPlan.whyShowThis: On the earnings side too, Muslim regular-salaried workers earn about ₹17,700 a month against ₹21,300 for Hindu workers, with Christian and Jain workers higher still.
chartPlan.howToRead: Bars represent average monthly wage for regular salaried workers by religion. The Hindu bar is the reference.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: As with consumption, sample sizes for smaller religious groups are small. Also, occupational segregation may drive gaps.

chartPlan.title: The other half: extreme poverty fell sharply
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Rising top-end inequality and falling extreme poverty are both true at once—the bottom rose even as the top pulled away faster.
chartPlan.howToRead: The line drops from nearly 60% in 1977 to 5.3% in 2022. The steepest falls occurred after 2004.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: This is an absolute poverty line; it doesn't measure inequality. Also, the 2022 dip may partly reflect one-off survey effects.

visualizationDecisions.reason: Two lines over time best show the diverging methods; full history preserves the long-run trend.
Single line showing a gently declining Gini; full history is essential context.
Two top share lines; the long history back to the colonial era gives the 'starker than the Raj' context.
Wealth shares are most informative over decades; the post-1991 rise is the key story.
One snapshot of shares is enough to illustrate the distribution; a bar chart is direct.
Two lines over time show the narrowing (or not) of the gap.
Only three rounds; a simple two-line chart works best.
Fractile ladder is inherently cross-sectional; horizontal bars from poorest to richest are intuitive.
Two lines showing MPCE growth; the narrowing gap is visible in convergence.
State comparisons are best as horizontal bars sorted by value.
Caste groups as horizontal bars, ordered by average MPCE.
Religion groups as horizontal bars.
Caste wage bars; percentage gap annotations helpful.
Gender ratio bars; sorted by caste to show compounding.
Religion wage bars.
A single declining line tells the poverty story powerfully.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Think of a pot of money shared among 100 people. If everyone gets exactly the same amount, the Gini is 0. If one person takes everything and the other 99 get nothing, the Gini is 100. It's a single number that captures how evenly a resource is spread.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: The Gini is the most common way to compare inequality across countries and over time, but it depends on what you're measuring.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Imagine adding up everything a household spends in a month—food, rent, transport, school fees. That's consumption. Inequality in consumption is usually lower than inequality in income because rich households save a lot of what they earn.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: India's 'most equal' reputation rests entirely on consumption data, which misses the very top because surveys undercount the rich.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Income is what you earn from wages, profits, rent, or capital gains before taxes. If a billionaire's stock portfolio grows by ₹100 crore that year, that's income. Most household surveys don't capture capital gains, so they miss this top tail.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Administrative methods that include capital income show India's top 1% taking 22-23% of national income, a figure many find startling.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Wealth is what you own minus what you owe: land, gold, shares, property, bank balances, minus loans. It's the accumulation of past savings and inheritance, and it can grow passively. That's why wealth is almost always more unequally distributed than income.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Wealth concentration in India is estimated to be among the highest in the world; the top 1% hold about 40% of net personal wealth.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Take a household's total spending in a month, divide by the number of members. That's MPCE. It's a workhorse metric in Indian policy because it directly reflects living standards; whenever poverty lines are debated, MPCE is the raw material.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: HCES 2023-24 gives us fresh MPCE numbers, and the distribution of MPCE across fractiles, states, caste, and religion opens a window into inequality beyond the Gini.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Imagine you could buy a bare-minimum basket of food and essentials that costs about Rs. 40-50 a day in India. The World Bank draws a line at $2.15 a day (in 2017 purchasing-power-parity dollars) to define extreme poverty. Anyone below that line lacks basic sustenance.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: The dramatic fall from 60% to 5% shows that the bottom half's situation has improved enormously, even as the top has raced ahead.

proseRules: No calculations unless the evidence packet supplies the derived number

plannedCharts.title: Same country, two verdicts on inequality
plannedCharts.subtitle: Top 1% income share — World Inequality Database (tax + national accounts) vs World Bank (household survey)
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The whole debate fits in one frame: both lines try to measure the share of income going to the richest 1%, and they disagree sharply because they are built differently.

plannedCharts.title: By what people spend, India looks equal — and more so
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · Gini index of consumption (0 = total equality, 100 = total inequality)
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The 'most equal' headline comes from here: a consumption Gini that fell to 25.5 in 2022-23, among the lowest in the world.

plannedCharts.title: By what people earn, the top is pulling away
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Inequality Database · share of pre-tax income going to the top, 1922 to 2023
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Income tells the opposite story to consumption: the share captured by the top 10% and top 1% has climbed back to, and past, colonial-era highs.

plannedCharts.title: By what people own, concentration is extreme
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Inequality Database · share of net personal wealth held by the top, 1961 to 2023
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Wealth is where inequality is starkest: a small group owns a very large share of everything India has accumulated.

plannedCharts.title: How consumption splits, bottom half to top tenth
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · share of total consumption by group, 2022-23
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The flip side of a low Gini: even on consumption, the poorest half and the richest tenth spend strikingly different amounts.

plannedCharts.title: The richest fifth vs the poorest fifth
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · consumption share of the top and bottom 20%
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: A simple, durable gap: how much of national consumption the top fifth takes versus the bottom fifth, and how little it has moved.

plannedCharts.title: Town and country, and the survey behind the 'falling' claim
plannedCharts.subtitle: MoSPI HCES · consumption Gini, rural vs urban, 2011-12 to 2023-24
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India's own consumption survey shows the Gini falling in both rural and urban areas — the basis for the 'inequality is declining' story.

plannedCharts.title: The spending ladder, rung by rung
plannedCharts.subtitle: MoSPI HCES 2023-24 · average rural MPCE by fractile class (computed from the unit-level survey of 1,54,357 rural households)
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The Gini compresses the whole distribution into one number; this shows the actual rungs. The poorest 5% of rural India spends about ₹1,677 per person a month while the richest 5% spends ₹10,137 — on consumption alone, before income or wealth widen the gap.

plannedCharts.title: Spending is rising, and the town-country gap is narrowing
plannedCharts.subtitle: MoSPI HCES · average monthly per-capita consumption, current prices
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The reason poverty fell: average consumption rose for both rural and urban India, and the urban-rural gap shrank from 84% in 2011-12 to about 70% in 2023-24.

plannedCharts.title: Which India you live in matters as much as which class
plannedCharts.subtitle: MoSPI HCES 2023-24 · average rural MPCE by major state (all-India rural average ₹4,122)
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: A national Gini erases the map. Rural Kerala spends ₹6,611 per person a month — more than twice rural Chhattisgarh's ₹2,739. The gap between Indian states rivals the gap between countries, and it is invisible in any single national number.

plannedCharts.title: The consumption gap that runs along caste
plannedCharts.subtitle: Computed from HCES 2023-24 unit-level microdata · average MPCE by social group of the household head
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The factsheet never publishes this, but the microdata does: average consumption falls in a clean caste gradient. A Scheduled Tribe household spends about ₹3,614 per person a month against ₹6,148 for 'Others' — a 41% gap that no single national Gini reveals.

plannedCharts.title: And along religion
plannedCharts.subtitle: Computed from HCES 2023-24 unit-level microdata · average MPCE by religion of the household head
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The same microdata splits consumption by religion: Jain and Sikh households spend well above the Hindu average, while Muslim households spend about 10% below it.

plannedCharts.title: The same gap in what a job pays
plannedCharts.subtitle: Computed from PLFS 2023-24 unit-level microdata · average monthly earnings of regular wage/salaried workers (validated against the published ₹21,000)
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Consumption is the gap after the fact; wages are where it starts. Among regular salaried workers, a forward-caste ('Others') worker earns about ₹25,957 a month against ₹16,516 for a Scheduled Caste worker — a 36% pay gap inside the same kind of job.

plannedCharts.title: Caste and gender stack on top of each other
plannedCharts.subtitle: Computed from PLFS 2023-24 unit-level microdata · female regular-salaried wage as a share of male, by caste
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The two disadvantages compound. A forward-caste woman earns 84% of what a forward-caste man does; a Scheduled Caste or Scheduled Tribe woman earns just 63% of her male counterpart. The gender pay gap is widest exactly where the caste gap is widest.

plannedCharts.title: And wages split by religion too
plannedCharts.subtitle: Computed from PLFS 2023-24 unit-level microdata · average monthly regular-salaried earnings by religion
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: On the earnings side too, Muslim regular-salaried workers earn about ₹17,700 a month against ₹21,300 for Hindu workers, with Christian and Jain workers higher still.

plannedCharts.title: The other half: extreme poverty fell sharply
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · share of people below $2.15/day (2017 PPP)
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Rising top-end inequality and falling extreme poverty are both true at once — the bottom rose even as the top pulled away faster.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
